heading: Oberlin College
content: At the age of 18 - I found myself at Oberlin College in Ohio. I earned my own way there with a scholarship and a summer job. I literally had to earn my own plane fare and make all the arrangements myself. My mom wasn’t so much discouraging as fearful. It was more like I was going off to war than to college. I didn’t really notice how hands-off my parents were being until it was time for them to provide their portion of the tuition not covered by my scholarship. When I told them the school expected them to provide a portion of the tuition, they hadn’t said they wouldn’t, but they never said they would either. My assumption that they would share the burden turned out to be incorrect, so I took the debt on myself. In that first year I racked up an amount that seemed to me beyond what was repayable in a lifetime. ( I did repay it though! )

I was also floundering culturally. As you might imagine, my home life prepared me not for this elite college full of affluent and high-functioning young people. I had little faith in myself and struggled to concentrate. I was unsure what my focus should be but felt compelled to decide quickly since I would have to repay all this money. I did survive that first year, but my grades were not impressive, and I felt like a failure. So when I was back home for the summer, working, I decided not to go back. I later tried to go back to different schools a few different times, but financial fears would always defeat me. By the time I was secure enough to focus, I was already working in the tech industry, having taught myself from O'Reilly books, and personal projects.

My college experience wasn’t an entire loss. I acquired valuable learning skills and essential knowledge. I was introduced to important foundational concepts. Oberlin was a gift, and I’m grateful to have had the opportunity to experience it.

Many years later during the crash of 2008, and having relocated to Rochester, NY, to support my partner and his aging parents, I found myself unable to find tech work. I dreamt of being able to support myself half time at a "day job" and half with (he)art work. After seeing several job listings for part-time bookkeeper, I thought a community college degree in accounting would make that dream a reality. I did manage to finish that 2-year degree with honors. Unfortunately, I found that I couldn’t simply jump straight to being an independent bookkeeper and would need to “pay my dues”. I soon found out why people like me don’t tend to go into accounting. By then, the economy was healing, I had built a network, and was able to resume my tech career.
